3. Linear Regression

Two canonical goals of regression are

  1. Parameter inference ⇒ Unbiasedness, consistency, specification

  2. Prediction ⇒ Generalization error

    • E.g. ridge is a predictive tool, not an inferential one.

In reality we use regression for other stuff too.

For most of this chapter we assume the fixed design: is not random and only variation in is due to . is a function of the noise, so expectations are conditioned on .

Goal of linear regression: Find an estimator for that has minimum risk.

3.1. Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)

OLS solves

(55)

Let’s warm up with the 1D setup — we are interested in estimating and in where .

Proposition 10 (Univariate Linear Regression). The MLE estimator assuming i.i.d. Gaussian errors minimizes the least squared error:

(56)

where all quantities denote sample statistics.

is unbiased (check by taking expectation over ) and consistent (Theorem 6)

In multivariate linear regression, we have where and is an matrix:

(57)

where is the th feature vector. We identify where is the y-intercept.

Proposition 11. Under i.i.d. Gaussian errors, the MLE for is the OLS estimator3:

(58)

We have and

With heteroskedasticity/correlated errors, we have the more general form:

(59)

Remark 1: If you sift through the matrix multiplications, you obtain

(60)

where denotes the original matrix without a column of 1s appended. Hence if we shift coordinates so that the mean of and each feature is zero, then we can do linear regression with and get the same as above since and . Hence, the slope from regressing with an intercept is equal the slope obtained from regressing with mean subtracted without intercept.

Note for ridge regression, we should always center first to avoid penalizing the intercept.

Remark 2: We get where denotes orthogonal projection, and so the residuals are in the form .

A linear estimator is in the form . For a linear estimator to be unbiased, we must have .

Theorem 8 (Gauss-Markov). Assuming

  1. Model is correctly specified
  2. has full column rank (no multicollinearity)
  3. (exogenity)
  4. (homoscedasticity, uncorrelated errors)

Then the OLS estimator is the Best LInear Unbiased Estimator. Homoscedasticity is required for “best” only. If we further assume normality of errors and independence, the statistics for are tangible.

Best means that no other linear unbiased estimator has smaller risk (variance). I.e. the difference in their covariance mtarices is positive semidefinite.

is also both unbiased and consistent under the fixed design since its a linear transform of .

Proof

Consider a matrix in the form for a nonzero matrix . To remain unbiased, must satisfy . Compute the variance and show that it differs from the OLS estimator variance by a PSD matrix.

□

Remarks:

  • With Gaussian errors, OLS is actually the best among linear and nonlinear unbiased (MVUE). The reason is because the set of non-linear unbiased estimators is empty. See https://statisticalhorizons.com/is-ols-blue-or-bue/. For example, if we’re predicting intercept only with i.i.d. Laplace errors, the median (biased, nonlinear) is better than the mean.

  • Gauss-Markov requires uncorrelated errors with the same variance but the errors need not be independent (recall 0 correlation independence). Hence the class of allowed errors is broader than i.i.d. .

    • For example, say where and are asymmetric distributions of mean and variance . Impose the following dependence: 50% of the time let , the other 50% .
    • A more concrete example: let and . Same variance, 0 correlation, but not independent.

  • The remaining two assumptions of OLS: independent and normal errors, are to allow a sampling distribution of .

    • Note that for a multivariate normal, 0 correlation implies independence.

    • Why independence?

      1. If the distribution depends on third order statistics, then we need to know the dependency to compute the sampling statistics.
      2. We can apply Lindeberg-Feller CLT to show that the OLS is asymptotically normal. In particular, we have so it suffices to show that is a MVN.

  • (Covariance Interpretation) After centering coordinates and fitting without a y-intercept, we can identify

    (61)

    where the first term is the sample covariance matrix of the predictors and the latter is the sample covariance vector, i.e. the vector with th row equal to .

  • (Geometric interpretation) In 1D, the OLS solution is the projection of onto (as vectors in or more generally ). In multiple dimensions, is the orthogonal projection of onto the column space of (spanned by vectors in .)

    Like literally is the orthogonal projection matrix — we get .

Definition 14 (Correlation coefficient and R squared). For a general model we can define the coefficient of determination asx

(62)

when has zero mean. A negative means your model is worse at accounting for the variance than simply guessing the mean ().

If is 1-dimensional, then

(63)

For generally, we can define as the correlation between and , equalling .

Proof

(64)

□

3.1.1. Sampling distribution of

We have formulas for the mean and variance of . Since its a linear function of , which is a multivariate Gaussian (MVN) assuming Gaussian errors, so is the distribution of .

(65)

We can use this to do hypothesis tests on the slope.

For normal errors of unknown variance, we can estimate with an estimate

(66)

and we obtain over its estimated standard error follows a -distribution.

For nonnormal errors, we get this same distribution asymptotically.

Note of sampling error for with multicollinearity We have since the orthogonal projection is symmetric and idemptotent. Hence multicollinearity does not affect our in-sample fit. However, for predicting at a new point, say at :

(67)

The variance is

(68)

Hence if x_0 lies in a collinear direction (small eigenvalue of ), then the variance explodes That should make sense. Since is not unique, out-of-sample prediction shouldn’t be possible. In-sample is still fine because cancels out the ill-conditioning.

3.1.2. Omitted Variable Bias

If we fit for only variable, then we have some bias. In particular, our OLS estimate is

(69)

when the actual unbiased estimator should be

(70)

Effectively, we can write the noise as and conclude that exogeneity is violated.

If we have no noise, the fitted is

Omitting variables can flip the sign of an estimated effect (classic “Simpson’s paradox” type cases).

How do we circumvent this? We want to minimize . Random treatment attempts makes independent of all other variables, decreasing covariance.

Example 15 (Missing Predictor). Suppose the true function has two independent variables ( and ) plus noise, but we only fit one (). Then the residuals look like . If and are totally correlated we are fine. If they are totally uncorrelated, the residual is pure + noise which is just more noise. If there is partial correlation we get a systematic trend.

3.2. Ridge Regression

Ridge regression solves

(71)

The closed form solution is

(72)

We should center and first, fit ridge, and then reintroduce the intercept, so that we don’t penalize the -intercept. We recover the y-intercept as .

  • Multicollinearity: Ridge makes the matrix always invertible ( is positive semidefinite) and stabilizes multicollinearity. It inflates small eigenvalues, reducing the variance. The cost is introducing bias.

  • Claim: Some amount of ridge regression is good for reducing parameter risk when there is nonzero error.

    Example 16. Introducing some lowers both the parameter prediction and function prediction risk in linear regression. The two norms are different so the optimal is different.
  • Ridge regression is an example of shrinkage, any method that systematically pulls raw estimates closer to some central value (often zero, the mean, or a prior belief) to reduce variance and improve predictive performance. The idea is that we encode a prior belief of “sparsity” — shrink towards zero to dampen fluctuates of predictors with no effect, which improves predictive performance.

By Theorem 10, ridge regression is equivalent to a Gaussian prior.

  • Note on reporting standard error: the sampled slope is a gaussian with biased mean and smaller variance. We cannot say the actual slope lies in a confidence centered at the predicted slope. If we could correct for the bias systematically, then we get report confidence intervals for the actual slope. However the bias is unknown. Hence it does not make sense to report standard errors for ridge regression.

  • We can attempt to debias but that just brings us back to OLS. For example, we can do

    (73)

    where are the residuals, to get an unbiased estimator.

    • In this case, Bayesian regression with a Gaussian prior still let’s us construct credible intervals.
    • We get the same issue with predicting , but hey at least our risk is lower (if we choose the right ).
    • [TODO] so we can’t do confidence intervals with RR!? The only way to debias ridge is to undo it completely.
    • Ridge is a predictive tool, not an inferential one.

So ridge shrinks more in low-variance directions (small singular value) and almost not at all in high-variance directions. is the covariance martix, and we diagonalize it to get eigenvectors. Large PCs (eigenvectors with large eigenvalues, are barely affected.)

3.3. Lasso Regression

Lasso regression encourages sparsity, so we get a similar bias-variance tradeoff as RR but with variable selection (For intuition behind sparsity see, https://stats.stackexchange.com/a/159379/388865. The idea is that if we gradient descent, the loss function doesn’t slow down as we reach zero.).

  • Fact. If , Lasso selects at most predictors.
  • Combination of RR and LR is elastic net (tunable).
  • By Theorem 10, lasso regression is equivalent to a Laplace prior.

3.4. Generalized Least Squares (GLS)

For linear estimators of the form with (unbiased), we showed above that the Euclidean risk is

(74)

Theorem 9 (GLS). Assuming is positive definite (rather than positive semidefinite), the unbiased linear estimator that minimizes the risk is where

(75)

Extension of Gauss-Markov: The GLS estimator is a BLUE.

Proof

Diagonalize , where is diagonal with positive entries by hypothesis4. Then we have the Cholesky decomposition where . Let , , and . Then and because is invertible we have a transformed minimization problem equivalent to OLS:

(76)

where we found the optimal and plugged in .

The transformation above implies that the GLS estimator is the “best linear” estimator. It is manifestly unbiased. Hence it is the BLUE.

□

Proposition 13. From above we have

(77)

This is exactly the solution of the quadratic optimization problem for finding the MLE:

(78)

We could have started with the quadratic optimization problem as the MLE estimator and showed that it minimizes risk. I prefer to introduce the content the other way around, which made it harder to grasp lol. OLS is usually introduced via the quadratic optimization problem first and the risk minimization is explained through Gauss-Markov.

3.5. Random Design

In the fixed design, is deterministic, and expectations are taken over the noise distribution only. In the random design, are jointly random, drawn i.i.d. from some population distribution which we assume to be in the form

(79)

From here, we have two subcategories of random design:

  1. Random-design OLS: We assume is random, but observed exactly
  2. Random-design Deming: is fixed, but we have noisy measurements of .
  3. (Some combination of the two above?)

Definition 15. The generalization error is analogous to the prediction risk, except the expectation is taken over as well:

(80)

3.5.1. Total Least Squares (TLS)

There is no closed matrix form like there is for OLS. There are two ways to proceed:

  1. The likelihood of a single point requires integrating over all latent points on the hypothesized line/hyperplane. Assuming a uniform prior, this is equivalent to the next strategy.
  2. Treat as unknown variables and maximize over both and all . For a given , the is just the projection.

3.5.2. Regression Dilution

If we have errors in (random design #2) and naively proceed with OLS, the slope we get is biased! In 1D, we have

(81)

Adding independent noise in leaves unchanged but increases Var(X). In particular, the bias manifests as an attenuantion factor:

(82)

where is the variance of the true values, and is the noise: .

3.6. Feature Engineering

Many successful alphas on longer timescales are linear combinations of engineered features.
